pattern: Binary search on the candidate maximum x: a given x is feasible exactly when the stores needed to split every quantity into chunks of at most x (a ceiling-division sum) fit within the available store count.
hints: Guess a cap x on how many products any single store may hold, and check whether all quantities can be split into pieces of at most x using no more than n stores total.
The number of stores a quantity q needs for a cap x is ceil(q / x); summing that over every quantity and comparing against n tells you if x works.
Feasibility is monotonic in x (a larger cap only ever needs fewer or equal stores), so binary search the smallest feasible x between 1 and the largest quantity.

edge_inputs.description: two product types split across more stores than types
three product types, generous store count
only one store available for the only product type
exactly one store per product type, no splitting possible
one dominant quantity dwarfing the rest
store count equals type count, forcing the largest quantity to define the answer
far more stores than needed for a single small type

stress.description: random quantities with a store count between the type count and the max
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    m = rng.randint(1, n)
    quantities = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(m)]
    return [n, quantities]
